title: Why Millions of Americans Stay in Jobs They Dislike
subtitle: A new report reveals that many workers are afraid to quit because they fear losing their health insurance.

summary.en: A new survey shows that nearly one in four American workers stays in a job they do not like just to keep their health insurance. This situation, often called 'job lock,' has become more common over the last five years. Experts say that high healthcare costs make it difficult for people to leave their positions. When workers feel stuck, they are less productive and less likely to start new businesses. Many analysts believe that changing how insurance is provided could help the economy grow.
summary.zh: 一項新的調查顯示，近四分之一的美國勞工為了保留健康保險，被迫留在不喜歡的工作崗位上。這種通常被稱為「工作鎖定」的情況，在過去五年中變得更加普遍。專家表示，高昂的醫療費用使人們難以離職。當勞工感到受困時，他們的工作效率會降低，創業意願也會減少。許多分析師認為，改變保險的提供方式將有助於經濟成長。

paragraphs.en: For many people, a job is more than just a way to earn money; it is also the primary way to get health insurance. However, a new report suggests that this connection between work and health coverage is causing a serious problem for millions of Americans. According to the West Health-Gallup Center on Healthcare in America, nearly one in four workers who receive insurance through their employer are staying in jobs they do not want. This phenomenon is commonly known as "job lock."
paragraphs.zh: 對許多人來說，工作不僅僅是賺錢的手段，也是獲得健康保險的主要途徑。然而，一份新的報告指出，工作與醫療保障之間的這種連結，正為數百萬美國人帶來嚴重的問題。根據美國醫療保健西區蓋洛普中心（West Health-Gallup Center on Healthcare in America）的調查，近四分之一透過雇主獲得保險的勞工，正被迫留在他們並不喜歡的工作崗位上。這種現象通常被稱為「工作鎖定」。

paragraphs.en: This trend has grown significantly in recent years. In 2021, the survey found that 16% of workers were in this position. Now, that number has risen to 25%. Ellyn Maese, a research director at the West Health-Gallup Center, described the situation as concerning. She noted that anyone who wants to leave a job but feels forced to stay just to keep their health benefits is in a difficult spot. The problem is even worse for people with health issues. The report found that 41% of workers who have three or more chronic health conditions are staying in their jobs specifically for their insurance coverage.
paragraphs.zh: 這種趨勢近年來顯著增加。二〇二一年時，調查發現有百分之十六的勞工處於這種情況，如今該比例已上升至百分之二十五。該中心的研究主任艾琳·梅斯（Ellyn Maese）形容此現狀令人擔憂。她指出，任何想離職卻因擔心失去醫療福利而被迫留任的人，都處於兩難的困境。對於有健康問題的人來說，情況更為嚴重。報告發現，患有三種或以上慢性疾病的勞工中，有百分之四十一的人是為了保險而留在原職。

paragraphs.en: Why are so many people feeling this pressure? The answer often lies in the high cost of medical care. An April poll from the health research organization KFF found that nearly two-thirds of adults are worried about being able to afford healthcare. Larry Levitt, an executive vice president at KFF, explained that healthcare is currently the top economic worry for many people. Because of this, it is logical that workers are afraid to quit their jobs, as they fear they would not be able to find or afford insurance on their own.
paragraphs.zh: 為什麼這麼多人感到這種壓力？答案往往在於高昂的醫療費用。健康研究組織凱瑟家庭基金會（KFF）四月份的一項民調顯示，近三分之二的成年人擔心自己無法負擔醫療費用。該基金會執行副總裁賴瑞·李維特（Larry Levitt）解釋說，醫療保健目前是許多人最主要的經濟擔憂。因此，勞工不敢辭職是合乎邏輯的，因為他們擔心自己無法找到或負擔得起獨立的保險。

paragraphs.en: This fear is made worse by the current state of the job market. A Gallup poll from May showed that only 28% of American workers believe it is a good time to find a new job. This is the lowest level of confidence in the job market since 2013. When workers feel that finding a new job is difficult, they are less likely to risk leaving their current one, even if they are unhappy.
paragraphs.zh: 這種恐懼因當前的就業市場狀況而加劇。五月份的一項蓋洛普（Gallup）民調顯示，僅有百分之二十八的美國勞工認為現在是找新工作的好時機。這是自二〇一三年以來，對就業市場信心最低的一次。當勞工認為找新工作很困難時，即使對現職不滿，他們也不太願意冒險離職。

paragraphs.en: Experts warn that this situation has negative effects on the broader economy. Maese explained that unhappy employees are often less effective at their work. Furthermore, a healthy economy usually relies on people moving between jobs or starting their own businesses. When people are afraid to leave their current employers, they are less likely to take these risks. This lack of movement can slow down economic growth and innovation.
paragraphs.zh: 專家警告，這種情況對整體經濟產生了負面影響。梅斯解釋說，不快樂的員工通常工作效率較低。此外，健康的經濟通常依賴於人才在不同工作間流動，或是人們創業。當人們不敢離開現任雇主時，他們就不太可能承擔這些風險。這種缺乏流動性的現象可能會減緩經濟成長與創新。

paragraphs.en: Some policy changes have also played a role in this trend. In 2021, the U.S. Congress increased financial help for middle-income people to buy their own insurance through the Affordable Care Act (ACA) marketplaces. However, when these subsidies—financial support from the government to lower costs—expired in 2025, that assistance disappeared. Levitt noted that the end of these subsidies has made it harder for people to leave their jobs, as the cost of buying insurance independently has increased.
paragraphs.zh: 一些政策變動也在這股趨勢中扮演了角色。二〇二一年，美國國會增加了對中等收入者的經濟援助，協助他們透過「平價醫療法案」（Affordable Care Act, ACA）的市場平台購買個人保險。然而，當這些由政府提供以降低成本的補助金在二〇二五年到期後，這項援助便消失了。李維特指出，這些補助的終止使得人們更難離職，因為自行購買保險的成本已經增加。

paragraphs.en: There is a debate among experts about how to fix this issue. Michael Cannon, a director at the Cato Institute, agrees that job lock is a real problem. He argues that tying health insurance to employment creates gaps in coverage and limits the ability of workers to move freely in the job market. Cannon suggests that the system needs reform so that insurance belongs to the individual rather than being tied to a specific workplace. This would allow people to change jobs without worrying about losing their health protection.
paragraphs.zh: 關於如何解決這個問題，專家之間存在爭論。卡托研究所（Cato Institute）的主任麥可·坎農（Michael Cannon）認同「工作鎖定」是一個真實存在的問題。他主張，將健康保險與就業綁定會造成保障缺口，並限制勞工在就業市場中自由流動的能力。坎農建議，該制度需要改革，讓保險歸屬於個人，而非與特定工作場所掛鉤。這將使人們能夠在不擔心失去健康保障的情況下轉換工作。

paragraphs.en: Ultimately, the situation highlights a deep anxiety among the American workforce. As healthcare costs continue to climb, the fear of losing coverage remains a major factor in career decisions. Whether through policy reform or changes in the job market, many experts believe that finding a way to separate health insurance from employment is necessary to help both workers and the economy thrive. For now, millions of people remain in jobs they dislike, waiting for a time when they can move on without risking their health and financial security.
paragraphs.zh: 歸根究柢，這種情況凸顯了美國勞動力內心深處的焦慮。隨著醫療費用持續攀升，擔心失去保障仍是影響職涯決策的主要因素。無論是透過政策改革還是就業市場的變動，許多專家認為，設法將健康保險與就業脫鉤，對於幫助勞工與經濟共同繁榮是必要的。目前，數百萬人仍留在他們不喜歡的工作崗位上，等待著一個能夠在不危及健康與財務安全的情況下，邁向下一階段的時機。

keyTerms.noteZh: 常見作名詞（地點、斑點），這裡指代困難的處境。文中：She noted that anyone who wants to leave a job but feels forced to stay just to keep their health benefits is in a difficult spot.

keyTerms.definitionEn: To be caused by or to exist in something.
keyTerms.noteZh: 常見作動詞（躺），這裡與介詞搭配表示原因或根源。文中：Why are so many people feeling this pressure? The answer often lies in the high cost of medical care.

keyTerms.definitionEn: To leave one's current situation or job to start something new.
keyTerms.noteZh: 常見作「繼續進行（下一個話題）」，這裡指離開現職尋求新機會。文中：For now, millions of people remain in jobs they dislike, waiting for a time when they can move on without risking their health and financial security.

sources.name: NPR Business
sources.title: Staying in a job for the health insurance? About 1 in 4 Americans do, a survey says

quiz.question: According to the report, what percentage of workers with multiple chronic health conditions remain in their jobs primarily for insurance benefits?
quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 D。根據文章第二段，報告明確指出 41% 患有三種或以上慢性疾病的員工，是為了保險才留在現職。選項 A 是 2021 年所有員工受影響的比例；選項 B 是目前整體受「工作鎖定」影響的員工比例；選項 C 則是認為目前適合找工作的員工比例，皆非題目所問的特定族群數據。

quiz.question: Based on the article, why did the expiration of government subsidies in 2025 likely worsen the 'job lock' phenomenon?

options.A: It forced companies to provide fewer health benefits to their employees.
options.B: It made individual health insurance plans significantly more expensive for workers.
options.C: It caused the job market to shrink, leaving fewer opportunities for workers.
options.D: It discouraged people from starting their own businesses due to new taxes.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 B。文章第六段提到，政府在 2025 年終止了對中產階級購買私人保險的補貼，導致自行購買保險的成本增加。這使得員工更難離開工作崗位，因為他們無法負擔昂貴的個人保險。選項 A 與文意不符；選項 C 雖提到市場狀況，但補貼過期主要影響的是「個人購買能力」；選項 D 未在文中提及。

quiz.question: In the final paragraph, what does the word 'thrive' mean in the context of the sentence: '...helping both workers and the economy thrive'?

options.B: To decrease in size or importance.
options.C: To grow, develop, or be successful.
options.D: To wait patiently for a better opportunity.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 C。在文中 'thrive' 指的是經濟與勞工在脫離「工作鎖定」後，能獲得蓬勃發展與成功。選項 A 'survive' 僅代表生存，語氣較弱；選項 B 'decrease' 是反義詞；選項 D 'wait' 與該詞的原意無關。'Thrive' 在此處強調的是正向的成長與繁榮。

quiz.question: What is the central message of the article regarding the relationship between employment and health insurance?

options.A: The U.S. government should mandate that all companies provide full health coverage.
options.B: Most American workers prefer to stay in their current jobs regardless of insurance.
options.C: The current system of linking health insurance to employment restricts worker mobility and economic health.
options.D: The job market is currently in its best state since 2013, encouraging workers to switch jobs.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 C。全文核心在於探討「工作鎖定」（job lock）現象，指出將醫療保險與工作綁定會導致員工不敢離職、降低經濟流動性與創新，因此專家建議應將保險與工作脫鉤。選項 A 為作者引述的觀點之一，非全文主旨；選項 B 與文意相反；選項 D 錯誤，文章指出目前對市場的信心是 2013 年以來最低的。

aiDisclaimer: 本文由 AI 綜合多家報導生成，事實請以原始來源為準。
